title: Word Problems: Rate, Work, Mixture & Interest
learning_objectives: Use distance = rate × time, including total-time and relative-speed setups.
Solve work problems by adding rates (jobs per unit time).
Set up mixture/concentration problems by tracking the pure quantity.
Compute simple interest with I = P·r·t.

pedagogical_model.sequence: I do → we do → you do

items.prompt: A car goes 180 miles in 3 hours. What is its speed?
Find distance if rate = 50 mph and time = 4 h.
If a worker finishes a job in 6 hours, what fraction is done in 1 hour?
Two cars approach each other at 40 and 60 mph. How fast does the gap close?
items.answer: 100 mph — speeds add when moving toward each other.

retrieval_starter.purpose: Retrieves D = rt, work-as-rate, percent-of, and relative speed — the four setups behind today's classic word-problem types.
retrieval_starter.if_students_struggle: If D = rt is shaky, drill the triangle (cover the unknown to get the formula) before composite problems.

questions.prompt: A car goes 180 miles in 3 hours. What is its speed?

opening.prior_knowledge_bridge: Every word problem today is a translation exercise built on one relationship you already know: rate × time = amount (miles, jobs, dollars of interest). The art is choosing a variable, writing one honest equation, and solving it with your linear-equation skills. Q5's relative-speed idea unlocks the trickier travel problems.
opening.learning_intention: By the end you can translate rate, work, mixture, and interest scenarios into equations and solve them.
success_criteria: I can set up and solve distance = rate × time problems, including total time.
I can add work rates to find combined or individual times.
I can track the pure quantity in a mixture and compute simple interest.

opening_script.move: Check; surface relative speed from Q5.
Bridge: all types are rate × time = amount; pick a variable, write one equation.
I do: two pipes filling a tank together.

i_do.stem: Pipe A fills a tank in 4 hours and pipe B fills the same tank in 6 hours. Working together, how long do they take to fill the tank?
i_do.explanation: Add rates: 1/4 + 1/6 = 3/12 + 2/12 = 5/12 of the tank per hour. Time = 1 ÷ (5/12) = 12/5 = 2.4 hours.
think_aloud_steps: Convert each time to a rate: A does 1/4 tank per hour, B does 1/6.
Working together, rates ADD: 1/4 + 1/6 = 5/12 tank per hour.
Time = total work ÷ rate = 1 ÷ (5/12) = 12/5 = 2.4 hours (2 h 24 min).
i_do.key_takeaway: Work problems: add RATES (jobs per hour), never times. Combined time = 1 ÷ (sum of rates).

we_do.stem: A cyclist rides 60 miles at a steady 15 mph. How long does the trip take?
scaffold_hints: Rearrange d = rt to t = d/r.

immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: Divide distance by rate; don't multiply.

we_do.stem: What is the simple interest on $500 at 6% per year for 2 years?
scaffold_hints: Simple interest I = principal × rate × time.

immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: The question asks for interest only, not the new balance ($560).

you_do.stem: A car travels 150 miles at 50 mph, then 120 miles at 40 mph. What is the total travel time?
Two workers together paint a fence in 3 hours. One of them alone would take 5 hours. How long would the other take alone?
you_do.explanation: Combined rate 1/3; one is 1/5; the other = 1/3 − 1/5 = 2/15 fence/hour → time = 15/2 = 7.5 hours.

you_do.stem: How many liters of pure water must be added to 10 liters of a 40% salt solution to dilute it to a 25% solution?
you_do.explanation: Salt stays 0.40 × 10 = 4 L. Need 4/(10 + x) = 0.25 → 10 + x = 16 → x = 6 liters.

you_do.stem: A boat travels 30 miles downstream in 2 hours and returns the same 30 miles upstream in 3 hours. What is the speed of the boat in still water?
you_do.explanation: Downstream b + c = 30/2 = 15; upstream b − c = 30/3 = 10. Adding: 2b = 25, so b = 12.5 mph.

sample_held_out_items.stem: A printer prints 24 pages per minute. How many minutes does it take to print 300 pages?
Sam invests $800 at 5% simple annual interest. How much interest does he earn after 3 years?

mastery_check.definition: A topic is mastered at >=85% correct on new/held-out-style questions across >=2 spaced sessions with delay (PRD Section 9.2). Flashcard recall alone is not mastery; application under mixed, delayed conditions is the bar.
conditions: >=85% correct on new/held-out-style items (not flashcard recall) per session
sustained across >=2 spaced sessions with a delay between them
per-topic memory surfaced only after >=20 graded reviews (PRD give-up rule)
per-topic performance surfaced only after >=8 application attempts (PRD give-up rule)
mastery_check.on_fail: Not yet: route every miss to the required error log (tag why: content gap / reasoning flaw / timing / careless / trap), schedule spaced re-practice via topic-aware scheduling, then re-enter the we-do -> you-do loop before re-testing.
